text: My presentation is about robotic lateral lymph node dissection using the data from Shizuoka Cancer Center Hospital. That's my disclosure. According to the Japanese guideline, the indication of the lateral pelvic lymph node dissection is the lower border of the tumor is located to the peritoneum reflection and the tumor depth is more than T three. In this indication, the rate of Lateral lymph node metastasis was reported at twenty percent by Japanese society for cancer of colon and rectum.

Then, this slide shows our strategy for rectal cancer surgeries. Low rectum and tumor depth is more than T-three, we do the bilateral lateral lymph node dissection. On the other hand, if the tumor is upper rectum, or T-two or less, or if patient underwent neoadjuvant chemoradiation therapy, we don't perform prophylactic lateral lymph node dissection.

The lymph node dissection area is divided into four parts. Area A is a common iliac node, and area B is an internal iliac node, and area C is an obturator node, and area D is an external iliac node. We usually dissect area A to C.

The video demonstrates robotic left side lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. The surgeon begins by mobilizing the autonomic nerves and the ureter, with the right ureter clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a common iliac node dissection, followed by the obturator lymph node dissection. The medial aspect of the obturator lymph node is carefully dissected, with visible mobilization of the autonomic nerves and ureter.

The robotic surgical view shows an irregular space between the interneuric arteries and obturator lymph node fatty tissue. The inferior vesicular artery is visible, with the dissection plane reaching the pelvic floor. The levator ani muscle is observed, along with the lateral aspect of the obturator lymph node dissection. The external iliac node and vessels are visible, alongside the pubic bone. The distal end of the obturator arteries and obturator nerves are also seen.

The surgeon dissects along the main trunk of the internal iliac vein, revealing the root of the obturator arteries. The obturator vein is visible flowing into the main trunk of the internal iliac vein. Dissection continues along the internal pudendal artery, exposing the coccyx muscle covering its distal end.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal end of the inferior vesicle vessels, revealing the white pelvic splanchnic nerve. The dissection plane reaches the pelvic floor, exposing the thick fascia covering the bladder and surrounding vesicular arteries and veins.

The surgeon completes the obturator lymph node dissection and proceeds to the internal iliac node dissection. The internal iliac nodes are situated between the autonomic nerves, including the hypogastric nerve and pelvic plexus. The second and third pelvic splanchnic nerves are visible, along with the distal part of the internal iliac node.

The video demonstrates a complex lateral lymph node dissection where a metastatic lymph node invades the main trunk of the internal iliac vein. The surgeon separates the internal iliac artery first, followed by the distal vein from the pelvic wall.

The surgeon separates and ligates the main trunk of the internal iliac vein, with numerous small and large vessels visibly flowing into it. The sacral plexus is also visible in the surgical field during this final step of the procedure.

And this is our consecutive two hundred ten robotic lateral lymph node dissection in Cesar Kansas Center Hospital. The tumor distance from the inner verge was only four point five centimeters and the tumor stage were almost three or two and four. and more than two-thirds of the patients were underwent bilateral lateral lymph node dissection. Even in this tumor allocation and tumor stage, only ten percent of patients were needed for neoadjuvant chemoradiation therapy.

For comparison of oncological outcome, the patient was stratified into the seventy-eight robotic and open lateral lymph node dissection groups. And this is those data. The local recurrence pre-survivor in robotic group was significantly better than open lateral lymph node dissection groups. In my conclusion, the robotic surgery is a good indication for lateral lymph node dissection. Thank you very much for your attention.
